channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Former Fauci Adviser Indicted on Covid-Related Charges
item.description: Prosecutors accused Dr. David Morens, a former adviser to Dr. Anthony S. Fauci, of hiding records related to the onset of the pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mueller
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Dr. David Morens, a former senior adviser to Dr. Anthony S. Fauci at the National Institutes of Health, in 2024 during an appearance before a House committee.

item.title: Nick Pope, U.F.O. Sleuth Who Chased the Truth, Dies at 60
item.description: Often likened to Agent Mulder from “The X-Files,” he worked for Britain’s defense ministry and became a leading commentator on extraterrestrial matters.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Thanks to GLP-1s, Obesity Experts Are Trying to Understand ‘Food Noise’
item.description: Before the rise of GLP-1s, obesity experts didn’t study the internal buzz that compels people to eat. Now that food noise is being switched off, they want to understand it.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Xia Gordon

item.title: 40 Years After the Meltdown, War Layers Another Disaster on Chernobyl
item.description: Ideas have been floated for how the contaminated zone could bring economic benefits to Ukraine. But for the foreseeable future, it will be an army-controlled security belt.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer, Evelina Riabenko and Brendan Hoffman
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times

item.title: Targeted Hunts Were Supposed to Curb ‘Zombie Deer Disease.’ Now What?
item.description: In Illinois and other states, officials hoped that culls could halt the progress of chronic wasting disease. Now they are losing hope.
item.dc:creator: Jim Robbins and Julia Rendleman
item.media:credit: Julia Rendleman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caught on a trail cam on the property of the photographer Julia Rendleman in Makanda, Ill.

item.title: Trump Fires Board Members of Group That Oversees U.S. Science Funding
item.description: The dismissals from an independent board that oversees the National Science Foundation marked the president’s latest assault on scientific research organizations.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Alex Wong/Getty Images
item.media:description: The National Science Foundation in Arlington, Va.

item.title: Trump Seeks to Abolish Iran’s Nuclear Stockpile, a Problem He Helped Create
item.description: President Trump withdrew from the Obama-era nuclear accord in 2018, saying it was the worst deal ever. But Iran responded with an enrichment spree that haunts the negotiations to this day.
item.dc:creator: William J. Broad and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: In February in preparation for a possible war with the United States, Iran moved missile launchers into positions within striking distance of Israeli and American military forces.

item.title: Were Neanderthals Able to Hunt Elephants? The Proof Is in an Ancient Bone
item.description: A new study found that a pachyderm skeleton, dismissed for decades as unimportant, offers evidence of careful planning, teamwork and a calculated kill.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: Could an Earthly Fungus Contaminate Mars? NASA May Have Found One Hardy Enough.
item.description: Researchers identified a species that can survive radiation, extreme heat and simulated Martian soil, posing a new challenge for protocols intended to keep the red planet pristine.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:credit: NASA/JPL-CalTech, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The scientists’ findings reveal that, unless NASA’s cleaning protocols change, exploration of the surface of Mars could inadvertently contaminate the planet with hyper-resilient colonizing spores.

item.title: Global Deforestation Slows, W.R.I. Report Finds. But Wildfires Are Taking a Toll.
item.description: In 2025, the world razed less forest than any other year in the last decade. The bad news: global warming is making wildfires more frequent and intense.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Harry Stevens
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Could At-Home Brain Stimulation Reduce Psychiatry’s Reliance on S.S.R.I.s?
item.description: A headset recently approved by the Food and Drug Administration uses a weak electric current to shock the brain. Some researchers hope it could challenge the current pill-centric paradigm.
item.dc:creator: Rachel E. Gross
item.media:credit: Joshua Bright for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sophie Davies, wearing her transcranial direct-current stimulation headset at home in Suffolk, England, to manage her depression and obsessive-compulsive disorder.

item.title: Pregnancy With Lupus Is Risky. Would She Be Able to Carry Her Baby to Term?
item.description: Fatimah Shepherd’s kidneys were compromised, and pregnancy could send her into kidney failure.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin and Heather Khalifa
item.media:credit: Heather Khalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fatimah Shepherd, a New York City Fire Department dispatcher, lives with lupus, an autoimmune disease that wreaks havoc on the kidneys and disproportionately affects Black, Hispanic and Asian women.

item.title: 60 Countries to Meet on Phasing Out Fossil Fuels but Are Excluding the U.S.
item.description: The Trump administration was not invited to the gathering in Santa Marta, Colombia. A White House spokeswoman called the green transition “destructive.”
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Fernando Llano/Associated Press
item.media:description: Dutch and Colombian environment officials at last year’s U.N. climate summit in Belém, Brazil, where this week’s First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels in Santa Marta was announced.

item.title: Immaculate Wilderness, Uncertain Future: Paddling the Boundary Waters
item.description: A proposed copper mine in northern Minnesota has become a battleground for politicians and environmentalists — and a pressing reason to explore the waterways.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Pearson
item.media:credit: Aaron Lavinsky/Star Tribune, via Getty Images

item.title: AIDS Creeps Back in Parts of Zambia, a Year After U.S. Cuts to H.I.V. Assistance
item.description: A once-robust H.I.V. treatment and prevention system, credited with saving hundreds of thousands of lives, has begun to crumble.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen and Arlette Bashizi
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Maureen Dhaka, left, with her baby in the neonatal unit at Arthur Davison Children’s Hospital in Ndola, Zambia.

item.title: A New Idea to Save the AMOC? Dam the Bering Strait.
item.description: Blocking the narrow waterway between Russia and Alaska could help stabilize a vulnerable system of ocean currents, scientists found in a study.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: NASA
item.media:description: A satellite view of the Bering Strait, which separates northeastern Russia and Alaska, in summer.

item.title: Golden Blob, a Mystery From the Deep Sea, Is Identified
item.description: The strange, shiny discovery, retrieved from the ocean near Alaska in 2023, turned out to be part of an anemone, scientists said.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: NOAA Fisheries

item.title: Doug Allan, Polar Cameraman for David Attenborough’s Films, Dies at 74
item.description: He was renowned for his skill at capturing candid scenes of penguins, polar bears and other cold-weather creatures — and for his ability to tolerate extreme discomfort.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Alan Peebles/BAFTA, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Allan in Glasgow in 2017 receiving a BAFTA Scotland award for outstanding contribution to his craft. It was one of numerous honors he received.

item.title: Trump Keeps Talking About Iran’s ‘Nuclear Dust.’ What Is It?
item.description: First of all, it isn’t dust. It’s Iran’s stockpile of near-bomb-grade uranium, which is stored in large canisters about the size of scuba tanks.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Planet Labs, via Reuters
item.media:description: A satellite image from February showing construction on buildings at the Isfahan nuclear site in Iran that were destroyed during U.S. airstrikes last June.

item.title: New Gene Therapy Enables Children With a Rare Form of Deafness to Hear
item.description: The treatment, the first of its kind, was approved by the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday. “Our baby was born deaf, and now he can hear,” said one parent.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Miles was born with otoferlin deafness, a rare form of deafness caused by a mutation in a single gene. But with a new gene therapy, he can hear.

item.title: A New Bureau Will Oversee Both Offshore Drilling and Seabed Mining
item.description: The new federal office will undo a change made after the 2010 Deepwater Horizon oil disaster. Critics say it could reduce environmental oversight.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Drilling platforms off California in 2021. The Trump administration said the move would speed approvals of offshore energy and mining projects.

item.title: Sewage Spill Is Over, But Contamination Lingers In Potomac
item.description: Though river monitoring shows bacteria levels have declined, scientists and environmentalists said a full recovery isn’t yet assured.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.category: District of Columbia Water and Sewer Authority (DC Water)
item.media:credit: Michael Noble Jr. for The New York Times

item.title: RFK Jr. Says His Department Advises All Children to Get Measles Vaccine
item.description: Testifying on Capitol Hill, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. continued to back away from his criticism of the measles shot. But he spoke on behalf of his department, not himself.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the health secretary, at the Capitol on Tuesday.

item.title: On Earth Day, House Cancels Vote to Narrow Endangered Species Protections
item.description: House leaders abruptly canceled a vote on the measure when support started to look shaky.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Jim Urquhart/Reuters
item.media:description: A grizzly bear in Yellowstone National Park in Wyoming last summer. Grizzlies are one of several species whose populations have rebounded in some areas in the past several decades.

item.title: Youth Suicides Declined After Creation of National Hotline
item.description: Suicides among young adults dropped most sharply in states that actively embraced the 988 crisis line, new research has found.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.category: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration
Journal of the American Medical Assn
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crisis line specialist working the phone at a mental health crisis center in Houston in 2022.

item.title: ‘Free Births’ Are a New Pregnancy Trend. Critics Warn About Serious Risks.
item.description: Promoters of free birthing reject any type of medical intervention during pregnancy or delivery. The movement has been trending on social media, but critics warn it poses serious risks.
item.dc:creator: Dawn MacKeen
item.media:credit: Bethany Mollenkof for The New York Times
item.media:description: When Emily Laszlo-Rath found out she was pregnant in 2021, she became intent on a “free birth.” “I just kind of started romanticizing the whole thing of being at home,” she said.

item.title: Pace of N.I.H. Funding Slows Further in Trump’s Second Year
item.description: The agency has approved far fewer new grants than it did in years past. A renewed effort to screen for disfavored terms and a loss of personnel are contributing.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mueller and Irena Hwang
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The main campus of the National Institutes of Health, in Bethesda, Md.

item.title: Dark Skies and Dark Energy Converge in West Texas
item.description: In the Big Bend region, a portal to the early universe is enabled by the largest dark-sky reserve on Earth.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller and Emily Elconin
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times

item.title: Musk’s SpaceX Goals Shift Ahead of Its I.P.O.
item.description: As SpaceX prepares to go public, Mr. Musk has proposed moonshots that differ from the company’s original aim of reaching Mars.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Ben Voldman

item.title: Does Clothing Recycling Really Work?
item.description: Collection services offer convenience, but most garments are shredded into low-grade stuffing or sent abroad to an uncertain fate.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Cernansky
item.media:credit: Mikayla Whitmore for The New York Times
item.media:description: A sorting facility for Supercircle in North Las Vegas this month. The start-up aims to help the fashion industry use discarded textiles to make new clothes.
